text: It might be hard to believe now, but
there was a time when Jos Weeden was
practically the king of Hollywood. A
talented writer and director with a
string of beloved TV shows and
successful blockbusters to his name,
plus the nerd credentials to make him a
fan favorite, the guy seemingly could do
no wrong. Until he did, and it turned
out he'd been doing it for quite some
time without any of us realizing. What
followed was one of the most spectacular
falls from grace that Hollywood has ever
seen and a strong candidate for another
episode of Crash and Burn: The Joss
Weeden Story. But first, some history.
[Music]
Joss Weeden was born in the 1960s in New
York City to parents who were both
heavily involved in the arts. His father
was a screenwriter and actor and his
mother an aspiring author and feminist
activist, which probably explains his
later focus on writing strong,
well-developed female characters and his
uh complicated relationship with actual
women. But more on that one later.
Anyway, Weeden's childhood could best be
described as difficult. His parents were
demanding and often verbally abusive
towards him and his brothers, and their
divorce left a lasting impact on him. He
was often bullied at school, especially
during a stint at boarding school in
England. And it was here that he learned
to be much more aggressive and
confrontational. Keep that one in mind,
too, because it'll definitely be
important later.
Anyway, it wasn't all bad. It was during
this time that he developed his passion
for writing, and when he moved back to
the States, he soon found himself
working as a staff writer for various
sitcoms like Roseanne and Parenthoods.
With a strong grasp of story and
character and a knack for writing snappy
dialogue, he soon became a popular
script doctor for big9s movies like
Twister, Speed, and Waterorlds. But
young Joss had grander plans. And while
he was doing all of this, he began
developing his own idea for a TV show
about a high school student who hunts
vampires in her spare time. That's
right. I'm talking, of course, about
Ronda the Immortal Waitress.
Gay. Okay, so the title needed a bit of
work, but this rough idea would
eventually go on to become Buffy the
Vampire Slayer, which started life as a
low-budget horror comedy starring
Christy Swanson. And well, the less said
about that one, the better.
[Music]
But the idea was good enough to land
Weeden a second chance with a TV show
that launched in 1997 and would go on to
become one of the biggest hits of the
late '9s, winning a bunch of awards and
even spawning its own spin-off, Angel.
All of Weeden's creative trademarks were
on full display, his complex character
work, his strong focus on female
empowerment, his ability to balance
humor and witty dialogue with moments of
crushing drama and posthos. Buffy
officially put Jos Weeden on the map.
Unfortunately, his next project wouldn't
be quite so successful. Firefly was a
kind of neoestern sci-fi series about a
crew of smugglers and mercenaries caught
up in an interstellar civil war.
Weeden's writing was still on top form.
And although it developed a strong cult
following, strong competition from
similar shows and a lack of network
support ultimately sank it. Firefly
turned out to be the right show at the
wrong time and never quite broke through
into mainstream success. Even though
it's now considered one of the best
sci-fi series ever made. This is why we
can't have nice things. It was a setback
for sure, but Weeden emerged from it
mostly unscathed and bounced back with
the horror satire Cabin in the Woods.
[Music]
But bigger things were a foot for Joss.
And in 2010, it was announced that he'd
been brought in by Marvel to write and
direct the first Avengers movie for
their fledgling MCU. And well, I think
we know how that one went. The Avengers
turned out to be everything Marvel
needed at the time, earning rave reviews
and overwhelming audience support, and
more importantly, raking in $1.5 billion
for the company. Almost overnight, the
MCU went from a disjointed and uneven
bunch of superhero movies to a global
juggernaut that's still going today.
Well, more or less. Weeden by now was at
the height of his success. Lordied as
the new golden boy of Hollywood, able to
turn his hand to almost anything and
most importantly transforming the way
dialogue was written for film. A process
which is now referred to as
Weedenization. But more on that later.
Marvel were happy and so were the fans.
And when it came time for the next
Avengers movie, the solution was
obvious. Hey there, old chap. Fancy
doing the same thing again and making us
even more money? Sure thing, Marvel. Age
of Ultron would prove to be a much more
challenging experience for everyone
involved. Though there were creative
differences between Weeden and the
studio right from the start, and the
film struggled under the weight of so
many conflicting demands, costing more
but making less than its predecessor and
getting decidedly mixed reviews.
Disillusioned and burned out after
working in someone else's sandbox for
the past 5 years, Weeden eventually
parted company with Marvel afterwards.
But it wouldn't be long before fate came
a knocking.
While the MCU was soaring to ever
greater highs, Warner's DC universe, on
the other hand, was struggling just to
get off the ground. The studio had lost
confidence in Zack Snider's vision for
the franchise. And when a personal
tragedy forced them to bow out of
post-prouction for Justice League, they
saw a chance to course correct. Hey
Joss, you know how you did that Avengers
thing for Marvel and made them a
shitzillion dollars? Any chance you
could come in and do the same thing for
this movie? Weeden reluctantly accepted
the offer, but it was pretty obvious the
film would need more than a bit of spit
and polish in the editing bay. A
full-scale rewrite and reshoot were what
was needed with very little time and
money available for either. To his
credit, Weeden threw himself into the
project, doing his best to give DC their
version of the Avengers, but it would
turn out to be a futile effort, and
Justice League would go on to become one
of the biggest disasters in superhero
history. It was a huge embarrassment for
Weeden, but even worse news was on the
way.
Remember when I told you about all that
childhood bullying which forced him to
become much more aggressive and
confrontational with people? and the
feminist activist mother who inspired a
complex and often difficult relationship
with women. Well, all of that stuff was
about to come back to bite Joss in the
ass big time. It all began in July of
2020 when cyborg actor Ray Fischer
accused Weeden of gross, abusive,
unprofessional, and completely
unacceptable behavior on the set of
Justice League. Jason Mamoa soon chimed
in too, referencing the shitty way they
were treated and that serious stuff went
down. Even Galgadot eventually pitched
in, claiming that Weeden was difficult,
demanding and verbally abusive,
threatening to derail her career if she
didn't do what he wanted. Now, this was
all bad news, but Weeden probably could
have survived the fallout if the
accusations were limited to this one
movie. After all, a tense and difficult
re-shoot on a troubled film that nobody
really wanted to be part of would surely
put anyone in a bad mood. But if things
were bad already, they were about to get
a whole lot worse. In February of 2021,
Charisma Carpenter launched an absolute
tirade against him on social media,
claiming amongst other things that he
was casually cruel while working on
Buffy, calling her fat, mocked her
religion, repeatedly threatened to fire
her, and eventually did. From there, the
floodgates were opened and pretty much
the entire cast of Buffy came out with
similar stories, especially the women
who seemed to have been particular
targets for him. Michelle Trackenberg
even claimed that she had a rule on set
never to be left alone in a room with
Weeden. One of Fireflyy's writers
revealed how Weeden used to boast about
making female staffers cry and made a
point of tearing their ideas apart with
particular venom. In the space of a few
months, Weeden's public image as an
affable geek, a principled male
feminist, and a talented writer with a
love of cinema was absolutely shattered.
In its place was an angry, embittered
bully who abused his power and made
everyone around him miserable. Pretty
soon, the mainstream media who used to
write fawn in articles about his
progressive characters and positive role
models turned on him with absolute
vengeance. He was removed as showrunner
from the HBO series The Nevers, and all
of his upcoming projects and public
appearances were cancelled. For all
intents and purposes, his career
basically ended overnight. An attempt at
damage control in 2022 backfired in the
worst way possible when he tried to
shift the blame to the Justice League
cast themselves, claiming that they were
rude and difficult to work with, and
that Galgadot just kind of misunderstood
what he was saying because her English
wasn't very good.
>> Hello. No.
>> Needless to say, it didn't do much to
help his case. Weeden had gone from
Hollywood's golden boy to a complete
pariah. Unable to find anyone willing to
hire him, unable to get a single project
off the grounds. In the five years since
this firestorm erupted, he hasn't
produced a single movie or TV show, and
that's not likely to change anytime
soon. These days, most people seem to
know him for his biggest and most
infamous contribution to cinema. The
dreaded weedonization of character
writing, making every line of dialogue
some ironic, self-deprecating punchline
to a not particularly funny joke,
turning every character into a snarky,
sarcastic pop culture quotation machine,
and draining all the drama and honesty
out of every single scene. which is
funny because what they're usually
pushing back against isn't Weeden's
writing at all, but some inferior hacks
lame attempts to replicate it. But it
doesn't really matter anymore because
the reputation is stuck and you'll find
few people willing to stand up and
defend the guy for anything these days.
Unlike the Rachel Zglers and the Brie
Larson's of the world who never really
deserved their success in the first
place, Weeden leaves behind him a much
more complicated legacy. a man of
obvious talents who did great things
with his career but also great personal
flaws whose success was ultimately
undone by his own [ __ ] up personality.
His career crash and burn might not have
been all that satisfying but ultimately
it was still very much self-inflicted.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away
